content.text: Why a Funnel Model for GenAI Adoption

Most enterprise technology deployments track a single metric: licence utilisation (seats provisioned / total seats). For GenAI tools this is not just insufficient — it is actively misleading. A tool that 90% of the organisation has access to but that 80% open once and never return to is not a successful deployment; it is an expensive shelfware investment.
The adoption funnel replaces the binary 'provisioned vs not provisioned' view with a six-stage progression that reveals
where people drop off
and
what intervention matches each drop-off pattern
. Without the funnel, every adoption problem looks the same. With it, a provisioning gap looks different from a retention gap, and each requires a different fix.

This document defines the six funnel stages for GenAI tools, shows how to compute stage metrics from platform telemetry, and provides a Python model for the full adoption funnel that drives the intervention logic.
The Six-Stage Adoption Funnel

The funnel runs from Aware (the broadest layer) to Advocate (the narrowest). Each stage has a precise definition — two organisations using vague definitions like 'active user' will report incomparable numbers. Use the exact definitions below to ensure your metrics are consistent quarter over quarter.
Stage Definitions and Data Sources
Stage
Precise Definition
Primary Data Source
Secondary Source
Aware
Employee has seen at least one official communication about the tool (email, Slack post, town hall mention) and knows what it is for
Comms platform send/open data
Onboarding survey: 'Had you heard of [tool] before today?'
Provisioned
Employee has completed account setup AND passed the mandatory compliance module; tool is accessible from their device
IT provisioning system + LMS completion flag
Tool login attempt log (first successful auth)
Activated
Employee has used the tool on a real work task at least once — not just a training sandbox session
Platform usage log: session with >= 1 non-tutorial interaction
30-day pulse survey: 'Have you used [tool] for a real task?'
Retained
Employee has used the tool on >= 3 separate calendar days in the most recent 30-day rolling window
Platform usage log: distinct active days count
Monthly self-report (for tools without telemetry)
Embedded
Employee uses the tool on >= 15 of the last 30 calendar days; GenAI is part of their standard workflow
Platform usage log: distinct active days >= 15/30
Manager corroboration survey (quarterly sample)
Advocate
Employee has done at least one of: referred a colleague to the tool, submitted a prompt to the artefact library, posted a use case to the pipeline
Repository contribution log + peer referral tracking
Champion interaction log: peer-referred entries

The Activated definition is the most important to get right. A sandbox training session artificially inflates the activation count and masks the real question: did the employee find a reason to open the tool in the context of their actual job? Exclude tutorial and onboarding sessions from the activation count by filtering on session metadata or by using a separate environment for training.
Computing Funnel Metrics

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class EmployeeAdoptionRecord:
    employee_id: str
    role: str
    business_unit: str
    # Provisioning
    provisioned_date: Optional[date] = None
    compliance_module_passed: bool = False
    # Usage (from platform telemetry)
    first_real_task_date: Optional[date] = None  # None = never activated
    active_days_last_30: int = 0                  # distinct days with >= 1 interaction
    # Advocacy
    prompt_contributed: bool = False
    peer_referred: bool = False
    use_case_submitted: bool = False

    def funnel_stage(self, as_of: date) -> str:
        if not self.provisioned_date or not self.compliance_module_passed:
            return "PROVISIONED_INCOMPLETE"
        if not self.first_real_task_date:
            return "PROVISIONED"
        if self.active_days_last_30 < 3:
            return "ACTIVATED"
        if self.active_days_last_30 < 15:
            return "RETAINED"
        if not any([self.prompt_contributed, self.peer_referred, self.use_case_submitted]):
            return "EMBEDDED"
        return "ADVOCATE"

    def days_since_provisioned(self, as_of: date) -> Optional[int]:
        if not self.provisioned_date:
            return None
        return (as_of - self.provisioned_date).days

@dataclass
class AdoptionFunnel:
    eligible_population: int
    records: list[EmployeeAdoptionRecord]
    as_of: date

    def _counts_by_stage(self) -> dict[str, int]:
        counts = {
            "AWARE": self.eligible_population,
            "PROVISIONED": 0,
            "ACTIVATED": 0,
            "RETAINED": 0,
            "EMBEDDED": 0,
            "ADVOCATE": 0,
            "PROVISIONED_INCOMPLETE": 0,
        }
        # "PROVISIONED" here means completed provisioning
        stage_order = ["ADVOCATE", "EMBEDDED", "RETAINED", "ACTIVATED", "PROVISIONED"]
        for r in self.records:
            stage = r.funnel_stage(self.as_of)
            if stage == "PROVISIONED_INCOMPLETE":
                counts["PROVISIONED_INCOMPLETE"] += 1
            else:
                # Employee is at this stage AND all earlier stages
                for s in stage_order:
                    counts[s] += 1
                    if s == stage:
                        break
        return counts

    def funnel_report(self) -> dict:
        counts = self._counts_by_stage()
        prov_complete = counts["PROVISIONED"]
        return {
            "aware":        {"n": self.eligible_population, "pct_of_eligible": 100.0},
            "provisioned":  {"n": prov_complete,
                             "pct_of_eligible": prov_complete / self.eligible_population * 100},
            "activated":    {"n": counts["ACTIVATED"],
                             "pct_of_provisioned": counts["ACTIVATED"] / max(prov_complete, 1) * 100},
            "retained":     {"n": counts["RETAINED"],
                             "pct_of_activated": counts["RETAINED"] / max(counts["ACTIVATED"], 1) * 100},
            "embedded":     {"n": counts["EMBEDDED"],
                             "pct_of_retained": counts["EMBEDDED"] / max(counts["RETAINED"], 1) * 100},
            "advocate":     {"n": counts["ADVOCATE"],
                             "pct_of_embedded": counts["ADVOCATE"] / max(counts["EMBEDDED"], 1) * 100},
        }

    def primary_intervention(self) -> str:
        r = self.funnel_report()
        if r["provisioned"]["pct_of_eligible"] < 80:
            return "PROVISIONING: Remove IT blockers; chase compliance module non-completers"
        if r["activated"]["pct_of_provisioned"] < 50:
            return "ACTIVATION: Run team workshops; assign champions to non-starters"
        if r["retained"]["pct_of_activated"] < 40:
            return "RETENTION: Identify why users stop after first use; run 'what got in the way' survey"
        if r["embedded"]["pct_of_retained"] < 30:
            return "EMBEDDING: Surface daily workflow use cases; run prompt sprints"
        return "ADVOCACY: Invite embedded users to contribute to artefact library"

# Example
today = date(2025, 6, 30)
funnel = AdoptionFunnel(
    eligible_population=500,
    records=[],   # populated from platform telemetry in practice
    as_of=today,
)
# Without records, the report shows only the aware layer
print(funnel.funnel_report())
print(funnel.primary_intervention())
The
primary_intervention()

method encodes the funnel's most important property: different drop-offs require different responses. A provisioning gap requires IT coordination; an activation gap requires champions and workshops; a retention gap requires root-cause research into why people stop after first use.
Measuring Each Stage in Practice
Stage 1–2: Aware and Provisioned
Provisioning data typically lives in the IT service management system and the LMS. Pull it weekly. Watch for two patterns:

Slow provisioning: employees who have been assigned a licence but have not completed the compliance module after 14 days. Send a targeted reminder with a deadline — the compliance module is the most common provisioning bottleneck.

Role-based gaps: certain roles (often legal, compliance, or executive) have lower provisioning rates due to heightened scrutiny or device policy issues. Track provisioning by role and BU, not just aggregate.
Stage 3: Activated

Activation is the most important conversion to watch closely in the first 90 days of a rollout. The benchmark pattern: activation rate rises sharply in the first two weeks (driven by curiosity), then flattens. The employees who had not activated by day 30 are unlikely to activate spontaneously — they need a targeted nudge.

Run the non-activator breakdown by BU monthly. A BU with a high non-activation rate typically has one of three root causes: inadequate champion coverage, a use case mismatch (the tool does not obviously help with that team's daily tasks), or a cultural barrier (manager actively discourages use, or team is risk-averse).
Stage 4: Retained

Retention is where most enterprise GenAI deployments fail silently. The day-30 active user count looks healthy; the day-90 count reveals that most of those users opened the tool once or twice and stopped. The metric to watch is the
day-30 to day-90 retention curve
— the proportion of day-30 users who are still active at day-90.
Retention Pattern
Likely Root Cause
Intervention
Strong day-30, weak day-90
Tool useful for one-off tasks but not integrated into workflow; novelty effect wearing off
Identify daily-workflow use cases; publish 'prompt of the week' for common tasks
Weak day-30 and day-90
Activation driven by social pressure, not genuine utility; poor onboarding
Run 'what got in the way?' survey; improve onboarding with role-specific use case demos
Strong day-30 and day-90 in some BUs only
Champion coverage or manager support inconsistent across BUs
Expand champion network into low-retention BUs; identify high-retention BU's practices
Stage 5: Embedded

Embedded users (15+ active days in 30) are the programme's most valuable asset. They generate peer referrals, use case ideas, and artefact contributions without programme prompting. Track them by role and BU to understand where adoption has reached self-sustaining critical mass.
Stage 6: Advocate

Advocates are rare — typically 5–10% of the embedded pool — but disproportionately valuable. Each advocate contributes to the knowledge commons (prompts, use cases) and generates peer referrals that are more credible than any top-down communication. Track advocacy by type:
Prompt contributors: submitted at least one prompt template to the artefact library.
Peer referrers: named as the person who recommended the tool when a new user signs up.
Use case shepherds: submitted or co-submitted a use case to the pipeline.
Community contributors: posted in the GenAI champion or CoP channel with a substantive tip or lesson learned.
Funnel Reporting Cadence
Frequency
Audience
Funnel Layers Reported
Key Question
Weekly (first 90 days)
Programme team
Provisioned, Activated
Are we hitting provisioning and activation targets on schedule?
Monthly
Steering Committee
All six layers, trend vs prior month
Where is the funnel leaking and what is the intervention this month?
Quarterly
Executive Sponsor
Embedded + Advocate counts; BU breakdown
Which BUs have reached self-sustaining adoption and which need intervention?
Funnel Benchmarks

Benchmarks for GenAI tool adoption vary by tool type, organisation culture, and rollout approach. The ranges below are drawn from enterprise rollouts of productivity GenAI tools (Copilot for M365, ChatGPT Enterprise, similar):
Conversion
Weak
Typical
Strong
Eligible -> Provisioned (day 60)
Provisioned -> Activated (day 30)
Activated -> Retained (day 90)
Retained -> Embedded (day 180)
Embedded -> Advocate (ongoing)

Do not use these benchmarks as targets before your first quarter's data. They are reference points for interpreting your own funnel, not aspirational goals set before you know your organisation's starting position. Set targets from your own baseline.
Segmenting the Funnel by Role and BU

An aggregate funnel hides the most actionable information. A company-wide activation rate of 55% may mask a Finance BU at 80% and a Legal BU at 20% — interventions that help one may be irrelevant to the other. Segment by at least two dimensions from day one:
By Business Unit

BU-level funnel data identifies structural adoption barriers. If Legal consistently has lower activation than Finance despite identical provisioning timelines, the root cause is almost certainly role-specific (legal staff have more AI-use restrictions, or have not seen a compelling use case for their work). The intervention is role-specific — not a re-run of the general programme.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date

@dataclass
class FunnelSegmentReport:
    segment_name: str       # e.g. "Finance BU" or "Senior Manager role"
    eligible: int
    provisioned: int
    activated: int
    retained: int
    embedded: int
    advocates: int

    def pct(self, num: int, denom: int) -> float:
        return num / max(denom, 1) * 100

    def weakest_conversion(self) -> str:
        conversions = [
            ("Eligible->Provisioned",  self.pct(self.provisioned, self.eligible)),
            ("Provisioned->Activated", self.pct(self.activated,   self.provisioned)),
            ("Activated->Retained",    self.pct(self.retained,    self.activated)),
            ("Retained->Embedded",     self.pct(self.embedded,    self.retained)),
        ]
        return min(conversions, key=lambda x: x[1])[0]

    def report_row(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "segment":           self.segment_name,
            "provisioned_pct":   f"{self.pct(self.provisioned, self.eligible):.0f}%",
            "activated_pct":     f"{self.pct(self.activated, self.provisioned):.0f}%",
            "retained_pct":      f"{self.pct(self.retained, self.activated):.0f}%",
            "embedded_pct":      f"{self.pct(self.embedded, self.retained):.0f}%",
            "weakest_stage":     self.weakest_conversion(),
        }

# Example BU comparison
segments = [
    FunnelSegmentReport("Finance BU",      eligible=120, provisioned=110, activated=88, retained=52, embedded=22, advocates=4),
    FunnelSegmentReport("Legal BU",        eligible=40,  provisioned=36,  activated=14, retained=6,  embedded=2,  advocates=0),
    FunnelSegmentReport("Operations BU",   eligible=220, provisioned=195, activated=130, retained=72, embedded=38, advocates=6),
    FunnelSegmentReport("Technology BU",   eligible=80,  provisioned=78,  activated=70,  retained=55, embedded=40, advocates=12),
]
for s in segments:
    print(s.report_row())
# Finance: activated 80%, retained 59%, embedded 42%, weakest=Retained->Embedded
# Legal:   activated 39%, retained 43%, embedded 33%, weakest=Provisioned->Activated
# Ops:     activated 67%, retained 55%, embedded 53%, weakest=Provisioned->Activated
# Tech:    activated 90%, retained 79%, embedded 73%, weakest=Activated->Retained (all strong)
By Role Seniority

Senior managers and executives show a distinctive adoption pattern: high activation (curiosity drives an early try), very low retention (no time to develop a habit), and high advocacy when they do embed (disproportionate referral power). Tailor messaging for this segment:
Don't target executives with 'try everything' messaging — identify two or three specific executive tasks (briefing synthesis, board report drafts, email triage) and route exclusively to those.
Assign an executive sponsor peer as buddy, not a junior champion — executives are more likely to adopt based on a peer conversation than a programme-led nudge.
Measure executive retention separately from the overall funnel — one embedded executive generates advocacy that propagates through their reporting line.
Funnel Diagnostics: Finding the Root Cause of Drop-off
Each funnel stage drop-off has a distinctive symptom pattern. Use this diagnostic framework before designing an intervention:
Drop-off Stage
Symptom
Most Common Root Cause
Diagnostic Question
Provisioned < 80%
Large eligible pool with low access completions
IT provisioning delays; compliance module too long or technically broken
Which step in the provisioning flow has the highest abandonment?
Activated < 50% of provisioned
Access granted but tool never opened for real work
Lack of a compelling first use case; fear of doing it wrong; manager not supportive
What did non-activators tell you when surveyed? (Run a 2-question survey)
Retained < 40% of activated
Opened the tool, used it once, stopped
First experience disappointing; no habit-forming task; forgot it existed
What was the first task non-retainers tried? Was there a common failure mode?
Embedded < 30% of retained
Regular casual users who have not integrated the tool into core workflow
No workflow integration; tool exists in parallel to standard process, not inside it
Which tasks do embedded users use the tool for that retained-but-not-embedded users do not?
Advocate < 8% of embedded
Power users who do not share
No visible contribution mechanism; no social recognition for sharing
Do embedded users know how to contribute a prompt or refer a peer? Is there friction in the process?
Handling Tools Without Telemetry

Not every approved GenAI tool provides session-level telemetry. Some tools are accessed via a shared interface (e.g. a departmental Copilot chatbot) or run locally without server-side logging. For these, use a lightweight self-report model:
Monthly 3-question pulse: (1) Did you use [tool] for a work task in the last 30 days? (2) If yes, approximately how many days? (3) What was the most useful thing you did with it?

Calibrate the self-report against available telemetry: if you have telemetry for some tools and not others, compare self-reported rates against telemetry-confirmed rates for the tools you can measure — this gives you a calibration factor for the self-report accuracy.

Triangulate with indirect signals: artefact submissions, champion interaction logs, and IT helpdesk tickets related to the tool all provide indirect evidence of tool usage without requiring full telemetry.

A self-reported funnel is less precise than a telemetry-driven one, but it is infinitely better than no funnel at all. Label self-reported metrics clearly as such in your reports — do not mix methodologies without disclosure.
Summary
The GenAI tool adoption funnel has six stages:
Aware
(knows the tool exists),
Provisioned
(has access and has passed compliance),
Activated
(used it on a real task),
Retained
(used it on 3+ days in the last 30),
Embedded
(used it on 15+ days in the last 30), and
Advocate
(contributes to the knowledge commons or refers peers). Each stage drop-off points to a different root cause and a different intervention.

Track the funnel monthly using platform telemetry for Provisioned through Embedded, and contribution/referral logs for Advocate. The most important conversion to watch in the first 90 days is Provisioned-to-Activated, because employees who have not activated by day 30 are unlikely to activate spontaneously. The most important long-term conversion is Retained-to-Embedded, because embedded users generate compounding returns through advocacy and knowledge contribution.
